text:
A Michael Kors Bag, Suede Slippers and More Holiday Gifts at Walmart â Starting at $9

A historic showdown between Team USA teammates unfolded at Bercy Arena in Paris.

The Olympic gold medalist credited therapy for helping get her to gold. During a press conference following her win on Aug. 1, the gymnast told reporters that her day began with a session with her therapist.

"Even this morning at 7 a.m., I saw my therapist, and there's a time change," she told reporters. "So she is so amazing for allowing me to do that these (past) couple of days ... I think you see that on the competition floor."

Biles has spoken openly about turning to therapy as a tool in recent years. During a2021 interview with TODAYâs Hoda Kotb, she reflected on how her struggles with mental health forced her to withdraw from multiple events at the 2021 Tokyo Olympics. She explained how therapy helped her to confront those challenges.

âI donât think people understand the magnitude of what I go through," she said at the time. "But for so many years to go through everything that Iâve gone through having a front, Iâm proud of myself.â

The Olympic champ showed off her gold medal and a goat necklace by Parisian jewelerJanet Heller Fine Jewelry.

According to apost from the jewler, Biles commissioned the three-dimensionalÂ piece adorned with 546 diamonds.

In the moments just before their medals were announced, video showed the two gymnasts waiting with bated breath in a nearly silent stadium.

As their results appeared, the crowd applauded, and Lee shook the American flag. Biles was the first to take to the floor before being quickly joined by her teammate. The two celebrated as their lively audience clapped and shouted from the stadium.

With a gold medal in her hand, Biles waved from the top of the podium during the gymnastics all-around medal ceremony.

Rebeca Andrade, Simone Biles and Sunisa Lee at the Olympic Games Paris 2024.ÂNaomi Baker / Getty Images

The 27-year-old stood next Brazilian gymnast Rebeca Andrade, who won silver, and her fellow U.S. gymnastics teammate Suni Lee, who took home a bronze medal.

Simone Biles and Sunisa Lee at the Paris Olympic Games 2024.ÂSina Schuldt / Getty Images

The gymnast took home the individual all-around gold medal for the second time in her Olympic career. Her win is thefirst time an American gymnast has won two golds for the all-around final.

Biles hugs her American teammate Lee after wrapping up her floor routine.ÂPascal Le Segretain / Getty Images

The two members hugged after competing in the Artistic Gymnastics Womenâs All-Around Final. The two gymnasts closed out their time at the games with a gold medal for Biles and a bronze for Lee.

The American gymnast and her Brazilian competitor embraced soon after Biles stuck her final landing at the 2024 Paris Olympics and wrapped her routine on the floor.

Biles snatched a 15.066 winning gold and Andrade came in second with silver.

Simone Biles has reclaimed her place atop Olympic women's gymnastics!

The Team USA star delivered a 15.066 in the floor exercise in one of the most pressure-packed performances of her legendary career to clinch the Olympic all-around gold over Brazil's Rebeca Andrade by a margin of 1.100 points.

She is now a rare two-time all-around gold medalist and the first since 1968 after having previously won it in 2016 in Rio de Janeiro. The 27-year-old is also the oldest all-around champion since 1952 and the sixth straight U.S. gymnast to win the all-around gold medal dating back to 2004.

Biles now has 6 gold medals in her Olympic career, the most of any U.S. gymnast, male or female.

"That's what greatness is," announcer Rich Lerner said.

"I can't believe it," Biles mouthed after finishing her routine.

Biles' U.S. teammate, Suni Lee, took home the bronze.

Biles has extended her own U.S. gymnastics record with nine total Olympic medals.

Brazil's Rebeca Andrade turned in a 14.033 in the floor exercise to secure at least a silver medal with Simone Biles now coming up.

Simone Biles supported her teammate, and now competitor, after her floor routine at the all-around final.

"I'm so proud of you," she said.

Her score of 13.666 meant that Lee will get a medal.

Martha Stewart got an enthusiastic shoutout from NBC analysts Rich Lerner and Laurie Hernandez, as did a few other celebs.

Skateboarder Tony Hawk was there, as was Kendall Jenner. Seth Rogen got a particularly excited reaction from Hernandez.

âNo way!â Laurie Hernandez said of Rogenâs presence. âThatâs so cool.â

Kendall JennerÂPascal Le Segretain / Getty Images

Tony Hawk.ÂNBC Sports

Seth RogenÂNBC Sports

Martha StewartÂNBC Sports

Simone Biles has the highest potential score in the floor exercise because of the degree of difficulty, so if she executes it well, she will win gold over Brazil's Rebeca Andrade.

Biles is trying to become the first two-time all-around Olympic gold medalist in women's gymnastics since VÄra ÄÃ¡slavskÃ¡ of the former Czechoslovakia in 1968.

Suni Lee is battling two other gymnasts, Italy's Alice D'Amato and Algeria's Kaylia Nemour, for the bronze medal with just the floor exercise remaining.

Biles, Lee and Andrade have yet to perform their floor exercise routines.

Simone Biles let out a whoop for Brazilian gymnast Rebeca Andrade during her beam routine.

Biles and Andrade may be battling for gold in the all-around women's final, but there was room for mutual support.

"They have enormous respect for one another," Rich Lerner said.

Online, commentators took note of the moment and applauded it.

It's all coming down to the final event between Simone Biles and Rebeca Andrade.

The Brazilian star turned in a 14.133 on the balance beam to put her in second place behind Biles going into the final event, the floor exercise. Biles and Andrade are both strong in that event.

Andrade was in first and Biles was in third going into the balance beam before Biles surged ahead with one event to go.

During Andrade's performance on the beam, Biles could be heard cheering her on in a great show of sportsmanship.

"They have enormous respect for one another," announcer Rich Lerner said.

Rebeca Andrade on vault.ÂCharlie Riedel / AP



Even Simone Biles herself exhaled after that one.

Biles turned in a 14.566 on the balance beam in a clutch performance that allowed her to jump back into first place for now after entering in third.

Biles' husband Jonathan Owens stood up and pumped his fist in the stands alongside Biles' parents and sister after her routine.

Simone Biles on beam.ÂJamie Squire / Getty Images

"That's exactly what she needed," Laurie Hernandez said.

Brazil's Rebeca Andrade, who entered in first, has yet to go on the balance beam yet.

There's one event left after the beam, the floor exercise, which is one of Biles' best events.

Simone Biles and Suni Lee are showing off different styles by wearing different leotards in the all-around finals.

In the individual competition, gymnasts are allowed to choose their own leotards, compared to the team competition two days ago, where they all wore the same ones.

Simone Biles and Suni Lee at the all-around.ÂGetty Images

They wore the same leotard to the team final.

Team USA coordinates.ÂLionel Bonaventure / AFP - Getty Images

Suni Lee has fought back into medal contention after a low score on the vault by turning in a huge 14.866 on the uneven bars.

The score put her in fifth place after two events. Simone Biles is in third heading into the balance beam.

"Smiled before she even landed," analyst Laurie Hernandez said.

Her family and a group of  young gymnasts at her home gym in Minnesota gave Lee a huge cheer after her finish.

Simone Biles had to take an extra swing around the uneven bars in her routine, resulting in a relatively low score of 13.733 that allowed Brazilian Rebeca Andrade to move into first place after two of the four events in the all-around.

"This competition has become a lot closer than I think anyone expected it to," analyst Laurie Hernandez said.

Andrade is now slightly leading Biles 29.766 to 29.499 after two events.

Biles scored a 14.433 in the uneven bars during qualifying, so this was a significant dip from that performance.

She had a decent-size lead after the vault, but is in a tight race after Andrade turned in a 14.666 on the bars.

Basketball star Steph Curry was spotted in the stands at Bercy Arena watching Simone Biles, Suni Lee and other gymnasts vie for all-around gymnast.

Curry himself is an Olympian. He'scompeting on the U.S. men's basketball team. This is his first Olympics.

The Olympics are an undoubtedly high-pressure event. But analyst Laurie Hernandez explained why the all-around can feel especially "scary."

"It's so much more stressful, going out there and not having your teammates on the floor. You can feel like an island," Hernandez said. She was not part of the all-around final at the 2016 Olympics but she did win a gold team medal.

At the all-around, it's every gymnast for himself or herself, whereas the team final incorporates up to five gymnasts' scores from a country.

Jonathan Owens, Simone Biles' NFL husband, is in the stands cheering her on and taking notes â literally. He's writing down her scores with a pad and Mona Lisa pen from the Louvre, NBC analyst Rich Lerner said during the broadcast.

Owens was able to leave training camp for the Chicago Bears, where he is a safety, for a few days to watch Biles. Heposted snaps from his voyage over, and was also in the stands for the team final.

At both events, he wore a shirt with her face on it.

@nbcsports via Instagram

Simone Biles turned in her signature Biles IIvault to start her competition, roaring to a 15.766 score that's higher than anyone else in the field to take the early lead.

"It looks like she's going to explode through the roof," commentator Rich Lerner said.

"The start value of that is so incredibly high that she has a cushion for a landing deduction like that," Laurie Hernandez said.

The Biles II is a vault that no other gymnast has ever done in international competition. Biles took a hop back on her landing, but the degree of difficulty is so high it didn't dramatically affect her score.

Rebeca Andrade, the silver medalist in the all-around from Tokyo hit a big score in the vault at 15.100 in one of her best events to start.

Andrade won the world championship in vault in 2023, beating Biles, who took silver.

Suni Lee started the competition as the first gymnast to take a turn on the vault, finishing with a solid score of 13.933.

However, Brazil's Rebeca Andrade followed with a great vault in one of her strongest events, most likely putting Lee behind early.

Lee sat down next to Biles during the long wait for her score, talking with her teammate.

"Suni is absolutely a gamer," analyst Laurie Hernandez said.

In the stands, Team USA gymnasts Jordan Chiles, Jade Carey and Hezly Rivera watched on as Suni Lee and Simone Biles were about to make their entrance.

"I can't â actually, I can imagine the amount of nerves they have watching their teammates," Laurie Hernandez, herself an Olympic gymnast, said.

A packed Bercy Arena is ready to go as one of the most coveted titles in all of sports hangs in the balance with the Olympic all-around finals getting underway.

Simone Biles is the favorite, but Suni Lee and Brazil's Rebeca Andrade should be right in the mix.

Given Lee's perseverance just to overcome akidney diseaseto qualify for Paris, analyst Laurie Hernandez said "any color medal for Suni Lee is such a huge feat."

The biggest warrior of the team competition on Tuesday was Brazil's Flavia Saraiva, who is back to compete in the all-around today despitesuffering a nasty black eyeon Tuesday.

Saraiva was warming up on the uneven bars for the team competition when she fell and smacked her face, giving her a shiner and a cut above her right eye. She got patched up by the medical staff at Bercy Arena with a bandage above her eye and still went out and competed.

Brazil's Flavia Saraiva is expected to compete in the all-around final after suffering a nasty cut and black eye during  a fall in warmups two days earlier in the team competition.ÂJamie Squire / Getty Images

Her performance helped Brazil win the bronze medal, which was the first team medal for its women's gymnastics squad in Olympic history.

Saraiva finished 11th in the all-around during qualifying on July 28. She will be one of two Brazilians competing today alongside star Rebeca Andrade.

Simone Biles and Suni Lee are looking to uphold a proud 20-year tradition when they compete for all-around gold today.

Thelast five women'sindividual all-around Olympic championshave been from Team USA. The Americans have had a stranglehold on the most prestigious individual gymnastics medal since 2004.

Carly Patterson (2004), Nastia Liukin (2008), Gabby Douglas (2012), Biles (2016) and Lee (2020) have all brought home the gold. The last non-American to win the all-around was Romaniaâs Simona AmÃ¢nar at the 2000 Olympics in Sydney.

The only other American woman who won the all-around gold besides that quintet is the legendary Mary Lou Retton in 1984, so Biles or Lee could make history by becoming the first two-time all-around gold medalist from Team USA.

In two of those years, American's finished 1-2, as Shawn Johnson took silver behind Liukin and Aly Raisman won silver behind Biles. There's certainly a good chance it could be another 1-2 finish today with Biles and Lee.

Simone Biles and Suni Lee are getting in the zone as they enter Bercy Arena in Paris wearing their headphones about 90 minutes before the all-around final begins.

There are more chances to see Suni Lee, Simone Biles, Jordan Chiles and Jade Carey before the gymnastics portion of the Paris Olympics wraps up!

The finals are still coming up for the individual events, so there's plenty more opportunities for gold for Team USA. Biles is a returning bronze medalist in the balance beam, and Lee is a returning bronze medalist in the uneven bars. Carey won gold in the floor exercise in Tokyo, but did not qualify for the floor exercise finals in Paris.

Here's the schedule and which U.S. gymnasts have qualified for each apparatus. All the events will stream live onPeacockandNBCOlympics.com.

Vault, Aug. 3, 10:20 a.m. ETâ Simone Biles, Jade Carey, Jordan Chiles

Uneven bars, Aug. 4, 9:45 a.m. ETâ Suni Lee

Balance beam, Aug. 5, 6:36 a.m. ETâ Simone Biles, Suni Lee

Floor exercise, Aug. 5, 8:20 a.m. ETâ Simone Biles, Jordan Chiles

The records keep piling up for Team USA's Simone Biles after winning team gold on Tuesday, with another shot at gold coming up today.

So how does she stack up against fellow Olympic greats like Katie Ledecky, Michael Phelps, Carl Lewis, Allyson Felix and others when it comes to gold medals and overall medals?

Biles, 27, is already themost decorated Olympic gymnast in U.S. historywith eight total medals, five of them gold.

Really no one compares to Phelps, the retired swimming legend whose 23 gold medals and 28 total medals are both Olympic records for any athlete in any sport.

Ledecky, 27, now has eight gold medals and 12 medals overall afterwinning the 1,500-meter racelast night. She is still competing in more events in Paris, so she could add to that total.

Simone Biles isn't too far behind Katie Ledecky when it comes to gold medals, despite having less chances to win them.ÂMaddie Meyer / Getty Images

Biles has a shot to reach nine career gold medals if she wins the all-around and the three upcoming individual events in which she qualified for the finals.

Swimmers have an advantage over gymnasts in that they have more chances to medal because they compete in relays and can qualify in numerous races. The maximum number of medals a gymnast can win in one Games is six, whereas Phelps won eight gold medals at the 2008 Beijing Olympics alone.

Plus, swimmers have more longevity than gymnasts. Phelps competed in five different Olympics, and Ledecky has indicated she will be gunning for a fifth Olympics in Los Angeles in 2028. The majority of gymnasts compete in one or two Olympics, with some rare stars like Biles competing in three.

The record for most total gymnastics medals is out of reach for Biles unless she defies time and comes back in 2028. Former Soviet Union gymnast Larisa Latynina is second all time behind Phelps with 18 total medals, 9 of them gold, across three Olympics from 1956-64.

However, Biles could catch or pass some other American luminaries. With two more gold medals she would tie track legend Allyson Felix with 7, and with four more she would tie swimming great Mark Spitz and track immortal Carl Lewis with 9.

The top 24 gymnasts from the qualifying round advanced to today's all-around finals, with no more than two gymnasts from each team by rule.

Scores from qualifying do not carry over, so everyone starts from zero. Each gymnast does one routine in each of the four events (vault, uneven bars, balance beam, floor exercise), and the scores from each are added together. The gymnast with the highest cumulative score wins the gold.

In each of the four events, gymnasts are judged on their execution and level of difficulty by a panel of nine total judges.Learn more here.

Today's action in the all-around final will put the world's top gymnasts to the test across four events: vault, uneven bars, balance beam and floor exercise.

Unlike the team competition on Tuesday, today's action will be gymnasts competing for individual glory, so they won't be relying on any scores from their teammates to get a medal. A maximum of only two gymnasts per team will compete.

Simone Biles is the strong favorite to take home the all-around gold because of her ability across four events.ÂLoic Venance / AFP via Getty Images

The all-around is a test of proficiency across all four of the events, rather than just specializing in one of them. Gymnasts who have a major weak spot in one event struggle to medal, while former gold medalists like Simone Biles and Suni Lee have shown proficiency across all four.

The scores for each gymnast in each of the four events are added up for a cumulative score that determines the winner. As an example, Biles had an all-around score of 59.566 in the qualifying round after adding up her scores in the vault (15.8), uneven bars (14.433), balance beam (14.733), and floor exercise (14.600).

If a gymnast gets the highest score in one specific event today, like the floor exercise for instance, they don't win a medal. There are separate individual finals for each event, with the vault (Aug. 3), uneven bars (Aug. 4), and the balance beam and floor exercise (Aug. 5) all still coming up.

Those upcoming finals can reward specialists who are really talented at a single event, whereas today is all about who is strong across multiple events.

Simone Biles didn't attempt one of her most breathtaking signature moves, the Biles II, during the team competition on Tuesday, but she will go for it during the vault to start today's all-around final, NBC analyst Laurie Hernandez confirmed during the competition.

Biles chose not to attempt it on Tuesday because Team USA is so strong that even conservative routines were more than enough to take home the team gold. There was no reason to risk a fall or a low score by pushing the envelope.

Today could be different as Biles tries to fend off challengers like Suni Lee and Rebeca Andrade as the favorite to win the all-around gold. The superstar, who hasÂfive gymnastics skills named after her, nailed a Biles II during the vault in the qualifying round on Sunday on her way to a 15.8, the highest score on the vault of any gymnast.

The breathtaking maneuver involves a back handspring off the vaulting table into a double pike, which is two rotations of the body in pike position.

She became theÂfirst gymnast to ever land that combinationÂ in an international competition at the 2023 world championships on her way to winning the all-around title. The skill has a maximum 6.4 difficulty rating.

No other gymnast in the world has ever completed one in international competition. The degree of difficulty is so high that even if Biles has points deducted for hopping back on her landing, it doesnât dramatically affect her score.

She also has a Biles II on the floor exercise, which involves two flips and three twists and requires immense power to get her high off the floor.

In case youâre wondering, the Biles I is a double layout with a half-turn in the floor exercise. Thereâs also a Biles I on the vault, which is a roundoff with a half-twist onto the vaulting table followed by a front double full somersault off the table.

Panamaâs Hillary Heron, 20, became theÂonly other gymnast to do a Biles IÂ in the floor exercise at the Olympics when she did it during the qualifying round on Sunday.

Simone Biles enters today as the favorite to win gold in the all-around competition after posting the highest score in Sunday's qualifying round.

If the 27-year-old delivers, she will be the oldest all-around champion in 72 years. The Soviet Union's Maria Gorokhovskaya was 30 when she won it in 1952. No gymnast older than 20 has won the women's all-around Olympic title since VÄra ÄÃ¡slavskÃ¡ of the former Czechoslovakia did it at 26 in 1968.

Simone Biles could add to the greatest gymnastics resume of all time with multiple golds in the all-around competition.ÂAytac Unal / Anadolu via Getty Images

A gold medal in the all-around or any of the three individual events in which she's competing over the next few days would give Biles the most Olympic gold medals of any American gymnast, male or female, in history. Biles is currently tied with men's gymnast Anton Heida, who won five gold medals way back at the 1904 Olympics.

If she swept all her remaining events and won the all-around, vault, balance beam and floor exercise for four total gold medals, that would match the Olympic record of nine career gold medals set by the the Soviet Union's Larisa Latynina.

It's the most of any woman in any Olympic sport, although U.S. swimmer Katie Ledecky also could tie or pass that mark during the Paris Games. The American record for a woman is eight gold medals by swimmer Jenny Thompson.

The superstar would become just the third female gymnast in Olympics history to win two all-around titles and the first since ÄÃ¡slavskÃ¡, who did it in 1964 and 1968. Biles won the all-around in Rio in 2016. Her U.S. teammate, Suni Lee, could also accomplish the feat if she wins the all-around title after winning it in Tokyo.

Biles is already the most decorated American Olympic gymnast in history with eight medals, and the oldest to win a gold medal at the Olympics.

One of the heartbreaking rules for Olympic gymnasts on powerhouse teams means Jordan Chiles will just be cheering from the sidelines today.

No more than two gymnasts per team are eligible to compete in the Olympic all-around finals. Chiles finished third in qualifyingbehind teammates Simone Biles and Suni Lee, who are two previous Olympic all-around gold medalists.

Team USA's Jordan Chiles did not make the all-around final due to an Olympic rule, despite finishing in the top four in qualifying.ÂNaomi Baker / Getty Images

Biles finished first among all gymnasts in qualifying with a score of 59.566, Lee was third with 56.132 and Chiles was only fractions of a point behind her in fourth at 56.065.

So even though Chiles performed like one of the top four all-around gymnasts in the world during qualifying, it wasn't enough to make it to today's competition.

A similar situation happened to Italy's Elisa Iorio and Brazil's Jade Barbosa, who were top 20 during qualifying but finished behind two of their teammates.

The good news is that Chiles can still go for the gold in the floor exercise finals, which are on Aug. 5.

Two days after joining forces to lead the U.S. to the Olympic team gold, Suni Lee and Simone Biles will be battling one another for the individual all-around gold medal today.

It will mark the first time in Olympic history that a pair of previous all-around gold medalists will square off against one another.

Suni Lee (left) and Simone Biles (right) will be going head to head with an all-around Olympic title on the line.ÂTim Clayton  / Corbis via Getty Images

Biles won the all-around gold in 2016 in Rio de Janeiro, while Lee won the all-around title at the 2020 Tokyo Olympics after Biles withdrew from the competition.

Biles is the favorite to become a rare two-time all-around Olympic champion after posting a score of 59.566 in the all-around during qualifying, the highest of any gymnast. Lee finished third in the all-around in qualifying with a 56.132.

After the U.S. rolled to the team gold medal on Tuesday, today is the day for the individuals to shine.

The all-around competition begins at 12:15 p.m. and will be shown live on NBC and streamed onPeacockandNBCOlympics.com.

The order of events is vault, uneven bars, balance beam and then the floor exercise. Since Team USA's Simone Biles and Suni Lee were among the top six during the qualifying round, they will be begin on vault and proceed through the next three in the order above.

Only two gymnasts from each team compete in the all-around, so Lee and Biles will be the only ones in action today for the U.S. squad.

Scott Stump is a trending reporter and the writer of the daily newsletter This is TODAY (which you shouldsubscribe to here!) that brings the day's news, health tips, parenting stories, recipes and uplifting stories right to your inbox. He has been a regular contributor for TODAY.com since 2011, producing features and news for pop culture, parents, politics, health, style, food and pretty much everything else.Â

Elena Nicolaou is a senior entertainment editor at TODAY.com, where she covers the latest in TV, pop culture, movies and all things streaming. Her superpower is matching people up with the perfect book.Â Previously, she covered culture at Refinery29 and Oprah Daily. She gotÂ her bachelor's degree in English with a concentration in Modern Greek Studies from Columbia University.
title: Olympics Gymnastics Final: Simone Biles, Suni Lee Accept Gold, Bronze Medals
description: Simone Biles and Suni Lee are competing in the Olympics women's gymnastics all-around final. Here are live updates on how they are doing.
